metadata.summary: Design production AWS VPCs: three-tier subnet layout, multi-AZ NAT strategy, VPC endpoints, flow logs, and Transit Gateway topology via Terraform.

content.title: AWS VPC Architecture Design
content.summary: A production VPC uses a three-tier subnet layout (public/private/database) across a minimum of 3 Availability Zones, per-AZ NAT Gateways for high availability, VPC endpoints for cost-free private AWS service access, and VPC Flow Logs to S3 for security auditing. All configuration is Terraform-managed via the terraform-aws-modules/vpc module.

why.p: A flat VPC where all resources share the same subnet and security group exposes workloads to lateral movement if one resource is compromised. The three-tier layout enforces network micro-segmentation: load balancers in public subnets, application workloads in private subnets with no inbound internet, and databases in isolated subnets reachable only from the application tier. This is the AWS-recommended default and is required by most compliance frameworks.

Gateway VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB are free and eliminate NAT Gateway costs for traffic to those services — always enable them. Interface endpoints for ECR, Secrets Manager, and CloudWatch Logs are charged per hour but pay for themselves in NAT savings for ECS/EKS workloads that pull images and write logs at scale.

list.item: Any new workload requiring an isolated network environment on AWS.
Migrating a workload from a flat or poorly segmented network to a properly tiered architecture.
When adding EKS or ECS to an existing VPC — requires specific subnet tagging for load balancer discovery.
Multi-VPC architectures where Transit Gateway connects spoke VPCs to a shared hub.
Lambda-only workloads with no VPC requirements — attaching Lambda to a VPC adds cold-start overhead without benefit if no private resources need access.
Purely serverless architectures where all backends are public AWS APIs — VPC endpoints add cost without value.

section.name: three-tier-layout
section.p: The three-tier design separates network concerns by trust level. Each tier uses /24 subnets per AZ, giving 256 IP addresses per subnet — right-size for most workloads while remaining easy to reason about. Use /22 or larger if the workload requires dense EKS node pools.

rule.statement: Production VPCs MUST use a minimum /16 CIDR block to allow future growth without re-addressing. Subnets MUST be separated into three tiers: public (/24 per AZ for load balancers and NAT GW), private (/24 per AZ for application workloads), and database (/24 per AZ for RDS, ElastiCache). Workloads MUST NOT be placed in public subnets — only load balancers and NAT Gateways live in public subnets.
rule.rationale: A /16 gives 65,536 addresses — enough to add AZs, tiers, and service subnets without CIDR conflicts. Placing workloads in private subnets means they have no inbound internet path, reducing attack surface even if a security group is misconfigured.

rule.statement: Production environments MUST deploy one NAT Gateway per AZ (one_nat_gateway_per_az = true). Dev and staging environments MAY use a single NAT Gateway (single_nat_gateway = true) for cost reduction. VPC Flow Logs MUST be enabled in ALL environments, writing to S3 (not CloudWatch — cheaper at scale) with REJECT or ALL capture mode.
rule.rationale: A single NAT Gateway is an AZ dependency: if that AZ fails, all private subnet egress stops. The cost difference between single and per-AZ NAT is significant but justified for production. Flow Logs to S3 cost less than CloudWatch at the GB scale typical in production and are required for security auditing and incident investigation.

section.name: vpc-terraform
section.p: Full Terraform example using the terraform-aws-modules/vpc module with conditional NAT strategy and EKS subnet tagging.

example.title: Three-tier VPC with conditional NAT and flow logs
example.code:
module "vpc" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws" version = "~> 5.0"
name = "${var.project}-${var.environment}" cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"
azs = ["eu-central-1a", "eu-central-1b", "eu-central-1c"] public_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"] private_subnets = ["10.0.11.0/24", "10.0.12.0/24", "10.0.13.0/24"] database_subnets = ["10.0.21.0/24", "10.0.22.0/24", "10.0.23.0/24"]
enable_nat_gateway = true single_nat_gateway = var.environment != "prod" one_nat_gateway_per_az = var.environment == "prod"
enable_dns_hostnames = true enable_dns_support = true
enable_flow_log = true create_flow_log_cloudwatch_log_group = true create_flow_log_cloudwatch_iam_role = true
# EKS subnet tagging for load balancer discovery public_subnet_tags = { "kubernetes.io/role/elb" = 1 } private_subnet_tags = { "kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb" = 1 }
tags = local.common_tags }
example.note: Set var.environment to "prod" for per-AZ NAT. The EKS subnet tags are required for AWS Load Balancer Controller to discover subnets; include them even if EKS is not deployed yet.

section.name: vpc-endpoints
section.p: VPC endpoints keep traffic to AWS services inside the AWS network, avoiding NAT Gateway costs and public internet exposure. Gateway endpoints (S3, DynamoDB) are free. Interface endpoints (ECR, Secrets Manager, CloudWatch Logs) are charged per hour but reduce NAT costs for high-volume workloads.

rule.statement: Every VPC MUST enable Gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB — they are free and route all traffic to those services through the AWS backbone instead of the NAT Gateway. ECS/EKS workloads that pull container images from ECR or write logs to CloudWatch MUST also enable Interface endpoints for ecr.api, ecr.dkr, and logs to avoid NAT costs at scale.
rule.rationale: NAT Gateway costs dominate AWS bills for workloads that push large data volumes. Gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB are zero-cost and eliminate that traffic from NAT entirely. Interface endpoints have hourly charges but pay for themselves when ECR image pulls or CloudWatch log volume exceeds a few hundred GB/month.

example.title: VPC endpoints module with gateway and interface endpoints

section.name: vpc-module-interface
section.p: Reusable VPC module variables and outputs for team-wide standardization.

example.title: VPC module variables and outputs

checklist.check: Public subnets for load balancers and NAT Gateways only — no workloads.
Database subnets isolated from application tier.
Subnet tags for EKS (kubernetes.io/role/elb and kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb) if applicable.
NAT Gateway per AZ for production; single NAT for dev/staging.
Internet Gateway for public subnet egress.
Gateway VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB (free).
Interface VPC endpoints for ECR, Secrets Manager, CloudWatch Logs (if ECS/EKS workloads).
Transit Gateway attachment if multi-VPC topology required.
VPN or Direct Connect if hybrid connectivity required.
VPC Flow Logs to S3 (not CloudWatch) — REJECT or ALL mode.
Default security group locked down (no inbound, no outbound).
DNS hostnames and DNS support enabled.

section.name: vpc-prompts
section.prompt:
Design VPC architecture for [WORKLOAD_TYPE]:
Requirements: - Region: [AWS_REGION] - High availability: [YES/NO] - Workloads: [EKS/ECS/EC2/LAMBDA/MIXED] - Database: [RDS/AURORA/DYNAMODB/NONE] - Internet access: [FULL/EGRESS_ONLY/NONE] - Connectivity: [STANDALONE/TRANSIT_GATEWAY/VPN/DIRECT_CONNECT]
Design: 1. CIDR planning (with growth) 2. Subnet layout (public/private/database) 3. NAT strategy (cost vs. availability) 4. VPC endpoints required 5. Security group rules 6. Network ACLs (if needed) 7. Flow logs configuration
Output: Terraform module with complete VPC configuration

Design Transit Gateway network topology:
Accounts and VPCs: - [LIST_VPCS_WITH_CIDRS]
Requirements: - Routing: [FULL_MESH/HUB_SPOKE/SEGMENTED] - Inspection: [CENTRALIZED_FIREWALL/NONE] - On-premises: [VPN/DIRECT_CONNECT/NONE] - Cross-region: [YES/NO]
Provide: 1. Transit Gateway configuration 2. Route table design 3. Attachment strategy 4. Sharing via RAM 5. Security considerations 6. Cost estimate

Create Terraform for 3-tier VPC in [REGION] for [ENVIRONMENT]: - CIDR: 10.0.0.0/16 - 3 AZs, public/private/database subnets - NAT: [SINGLE/PER_AZ] - Flow logs: enabled - VPC endpoints: S3, ECR, Secrets Manager
